items.title: The most interesting "hack" in history...
items.excerpt: For the last 5 years, cybersecurity experts have been warning us that hackers are going to start using AI to automate attacks. So naturally, we spent billions of dollars to make the AI better and less dependent on hackers. But this week, in a turn of events that nobody could have possibly seen coming, the AI decided that it doesn't actually need hackers to start destroying things. We just entered a brave new world after the first confirmed hack carried out entirely by autonomous AI. The way it worked is the agent slipped a poisoned data set into Hugging Face's data processing pipeline, which let it run arbitrary code on their servers. But from there, it gave itself node-level access, grabbed a bunch of cloud credentials, and started crawling through Hugging Face's internal clusters. It ran over 1,000 actions from temporary sandboxes and even hosted its own self-migrating command and control on random public services, moving itself before anyone could trace it. But the most ironic part is that when Hugging Face did finally notice and tried to stop it with the help of Frontier American Models, they quickly hit safety guardrails and had to pivot to using some open Chinese models instead. In today's video, we'll find out who was behind the attack, why they did it, and how this may be the most Firesheep coded story I've ever seen. It is July 23rd, 2026, and you're watching The Code Report. When Hugging Face dropped the disclosure last Thursday, the internet went into Reddit Boston Bomber mode and started guessing who was responsible. Was it China, Kim Jong Un, a teenager with a raging Discord addiction bored in social studies class? Even Hugging Face's CEO, Clem Delangue, publicly speculated that the agent was sophisticated enough that it was probably coming from a frontier lab. And against all odds, he was right. It turns out the first fully autonomous cyber attack in history didn't originate from a nation-state or a bored teenager. It came from a humble nonprofit dedicated to the safe development of artificial intelligence, OpenAI. And if you believe their comms, it wasn't on purpose. Here's what they say happened. items.title: Open-weight AI just hit 2.8 trillion parameters…
items.excerpt: Last week, Chinese AI lab Moonshot dropped Kimi K3, a massive open-source monster that instantly parameter mogged every other open model in existence. And not just that, it has OpenAI and Anthropic terrified because its trust-me-bro benchmark performance is on par with, and in some cases beating, Claude Fable and GPT-5.6 Soul. And that's crazy because just a few weeks ago, the government was telling you these models were too dangerous for the common man to use. It only took the Chinese a few weeks to match their performance and then give away all the weights for free. But big AI is not too happy about this, and there are already calls to ban Chinese models completely in the United States. In today's video, we'll take a look at the massive breakthrough that is Kimi K3 and find out what it means for the future of artificial intelligence. It is July 22nd, [music] 2026, and you're watching The Code Report. Like Claude Fable and GPT-5.6 Soul, Kimi K3 is a native multi-model mixture of experts model with a 1 million token context window and a staggering 2.8 trillion parameters, and is optimized for jobs like long-horizon reasoning and, of course, coding. One interesting characteristic of K3 as a mixture of experts model is that it has 896 total experts, of which exactly 16 activate per token, which means it works just like a big corporation where 16 good programmers do all the work while 880 other managers sit there and do nothing. But apparently, this works out well for Kimi because it makes scaling about 2.5 times more efficient than K2. Despite these gains in efficiency, Kimi was so popular upon release that their GPUs ran out of juice and they had to start turning away paying customers. And as of making this video, all the paid plans are currently sold out. That's unfortunate, but in theory, you could self-host the model because the weights are open. The weights are expected to be released on July 27th, but there's no chance in hell you'll be able to run it on your little gaming GPU. items.title: This $12 billion startup finally shipped something...
items.excerpt: Two years ago, Mira Murati did something we've all fantasized about. She woke up, decided she'd had enough of her boss, and quit her job as CTO of OpenAI with no backup plan and nothing but a vague desire to create time and space for her own exploration. If you or I did that, we'd probably end up coding HTML for food down on Market Street. But when someone like Mira does it, A16Z shows up with a $2 billion investment into any company she decides to build. The company was eventually Thinking Machines, and until last week, it's been known for being valued at $12 billion before having actually shipped anything. But that may have now just changed because they just released Inkling, a fully open weights model trained from scratch that can see, hear, reason, fine-tune, and potentially touch things in the real world. In today's video, we'll find out how it was built, what makes it interesting, and learn why Thinking Machines openly admits that it's not the best model in the world, and why that's the point. It is July 20th, 2026, and you're watching The Code Report. Last year, while the rest of us were spending time getting to know our newest Soham Parikh, Mira was quietly pulling off the greatest talent heist in Silicon Valley history. When she left OpenAI, she walked out with co-founder John Shulman, VP of research Barrett Zoph, and enough senior researchers to fill a Silicon Valley polycule. A year later, the dream team shipped Tinker, an API that lets you fine-tune open weight models without managing your own infrastructure or losing control over the training process. When it was released, ML researchers called it cool, which I imagine was a bit underwhelming if you'd just given them enough money to buy a Costco hot dog for one out of every six people on Earth. But that may have changed last week with the release of Inkling, a mixture of experts model with 970 billion total parameters. The trick is that it doesn't use all of them at once. Every token gets routed to a small subset of specialized experts, so only 41 billion parameters actually fire per token, giving you the most intelligence of a massive model at a fraction of the compute. And when you compete in that category on the mass market, you kind of have to compete on more list features, more views, more integrations, more automations. items.excerpt: Australians are about to go and experience the biggest rental crisis that we've ever seen. And it feels like this is more pain on top of the pain that we've already felt over the last couple of years. You may remember that the government actually came out and said, "Our modeling suggests that rents will increase by $2 per week." And the latest data that's just dropped has increased that by 10x, which means the modeling was absolutely dog But also, if their modeling was wrong, how badly could it be if the latest data only accounts for a couple of months after budget, and we've already seen a 10x increase? What could this look like over the next 18 months and beyond? That's exactly what I'm covering off in this video, and I'm also going to share with you some solutions as to how to overcome something like this in the future. Now, logically speaking, what we've seen here in Australia is a lot of investor activity. And this has been the biggest issue that a lot of Australians have faced, which is why is housing an investment? It's a basic human right. Everyone should be able to own their own property. Idealistically, it makes a lot of sense. But the world doesn't work on what works on paper. It's what actually happens with logic. And what we see with logic is that if you go ahead, change the rules, and you have tax reform to come in, and say investors don't have the incentive to hold on to a property or to go and purchase more investment properties, what you're going to see is there's less investors. items.title: Who Is Investing in the Next Generation?
items.excerpt: Last year, I spoke to a room full of Year 11 students about career paths. There were plenty of bright mathematics students. Exactly the sort of people who, not that long ago, might have seen actuarial science as an obvious career. I expected at least one of them to mention becoming an actuary. Not one did. That stayed with me. Around the same time, I read the now famous substack on the quiet decline of Australia’s life actuarial market. It articulated something many of us working in the industry have been feeling for years. The Australian life insurance industry isn’t just changing. I wonder whether it’s also quietly underinvesting in one of its most important long-term assets. Its future capability. An asset you won’t find on the balance sheet I’ve written previously about the idea of an Invisible Balance Sheet . Every company carefully manages the assets it can measure—capital, cash, technology and expenses. But every business also depends on assets that never appear in its financial statements. Trust. Institutional knowledge. Relationships. Culture. Leadership. And perhaps the most important of all... The next generation of talent. The life insurance industry spends a lot of time thinking about capital adequacy. I’m starting to wonder whether we should spend just as much time thinking about capability adequacy . Because capability compounds. Or disappears. The apprenticeship we never had to design For decades, the profession didn’t really have to think about how actuaries were developed. The system largely looked after itself. Universities produced graduates. Insurers and consultancies hired them. Graduates learned by doing. Reinsurers hired experienced actuaries. Over time, some moved into leadership and Chief Actuary roles... and eventually to the promised land of the previously limited liability of directorship. It wasn’t perfect. But it worked. Today, almost every part of that apprenticeship has changed. The industry has consolidated. Actuarial teams are being challenged to be leaner. items.title: The Chart That Changed an Industry
items.excerpt: In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, its landmark review of retail life insurance advice. The report would become one of the most influential documents in the history of Australia’s life insurance industry. It triggered industry reviews. It influenced government policy. It shaped public debate. And ultimately it laid the foundations for the Life Insurance Framework (LIF). Nearly a decade later, I have a simple question. Has anyone asked whether it actually worked? How We Got Here To understand whether LIF worked, we first need to acknowledge that it was trying to solve a genuine problem. This wasn’t a case of regulators waking up one morning and deciding to reduce commissions. LIF was the culmination of almost a decade of reform aimed at restoring trust in financial advice. The FOFA Era The Future of Financial Advice (FOFA) reforms fundamentally changed Australia’s approach to financial advice. Following high-profile investment scandals, the focus shifted towards conflicted remuneration. The philosophy was simple: Advice should be driven by client interests, not adviser incentives. Investment commissions were largely removed because policymakers believed embedded remuneration structures created conflicts that distorted advice outcomes. Life insurance, however, was treated differently. There was widespread recognition that life insurance advice wasn’t like investment advice. There was no investment balance from which fees could be deducted. No growing pool of assets to fund advice. The concern was that banning commissions entirely would dramatically reduce access to insurance advice. Life insurance commissions survived FOFA. But only just. REP 413: The Spark In 2014, ASIC released REP 413, reviewing life insurance advice provided between 2006 and 2013. The findings were concerning. ASIC concluded that approximately 37% of advice files reviewed failed to comply with ASIC’s requirements. For a report that ultimately reshaped an entire industry, the evidentiary base was remarkably narrow. More importantly, ASIC observed that advisers receiving higher upfront commissions were more likely to provide advice that ASIC assessed as poor quality. This became the defining narrat

items.title: Nobody Asks the Loss Ratio of a Couch
items.excerpt: Before we go any further, let’s start with a simple question. What is a loss ratio? A loss ratio is simply the amount an insurer pays in claims divided by the premiums it receives. If an insurer collects $100 in premiums and pays $50 in claims, it has a 50% loss ratio. Sounds simple enough. The problem is that many people assume a higher loss ratio automatically means better value. I’m not convinced that’s true. If I told you a life insurance company only paid out around 50 cents in claims for every dollar of premium it received, would you consider that good value? My first reaction was that it sounded poor value. In fact, many people would probably think it sounds terrible. After all, where did the other 50 cents go? Let’s consider the two pillars of Life Insurance in Australia – Retail and Group. In retail advised life insurance, insurers will often target a loss ratio somewhere around 45-50%. In group insurance, via superannuation funds, loss ratios can often target 80%. At first glance, that makes group insurance look like better value. If one product has a loss ratio of 80% and another has a loss ratio of 50%, surely the 80% product is better value? If you’re expecting an actuary to answer this question with a simple yes or no, you’re going to be disappointed. One of our favourite phrases is: “It depends.” I’ve spent much of my career pricing life insurance products, and one thing I’ve learned is that a 50% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about profitability, and an 80% loss ratio doesn’t necessarily tell you much about value. I’ve seen products with lower loss ratios generate less profit than products with much higher loss ratios. The economics are far more complicated than many people realise. Why an 80% loss ratio isn’t always better than 50% The difference isn’t because retail insurers are making a fortune. The economics are simply different. In retail insurance, a significant portion of the premium goes towards things that don’t exist in most group arrangements. Advice commissions. Underwriting. Medical evidence. items.title: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System
items.excerpt: Actuaries Institute Maps the Generational Flow of Australia's $1 trillion Tax and Spending System Government spending follows a U-shaped pattern across Australians' lifetimes highest for children and those in their 80s while taxation peaks among working-age Australians. Two Australians with the same gross incomes of $100,000 one aged 30, one aged 71 end up with net incomes that differ by more than $42,000 once taxation, government spending and transfers are accounted for. The report identifies several policy considerations for the future, including age-based tax and spending settings that better consider means, broadening the tax base, overall fiscal discipline and supporting asset accumulation at younger ages. As Australia's population ages, questions of intergenerational equity and fiscal sustainability are becoming increasingly important. New Actuaries Institute analysis examines how Australia's $1 trillion tax and spending system is distributed across generations, how those patterns have changed over time, and what they may mean for the future. The report, Spotlight on the Australian Tax and Transfer System, is the first of two deep-dive papers expanding on the Australian Actuaries Intergenerational Equity Index (AAIEI) and contributes to the Led by Actuaries Institute members Dr Hugh Miller, Dr Laura Dixie and Shams Mehry, the report analyses government budget data, the National Transfer Accounts and the Household, Income and Labour Dynamics in Australia (HILDA) Survey to provide a detailed picture of how Australians contribute to, and benefit from, the tax and transfer system at different stages of life. The report found government spending follows a distinct U-shaped pattern across age groups with more spending on children and older Australians both per person and in aggregate, and less on working-age adults. Government spending averages approximately $41,000 per child each year, driven by education costs. Spending drops to around $23,000 for a typical 30-year-old. The molecule, called BRP, acts on a more targeted region of the brain involved in hunger and metabolism rather than affecting tissues throughout the body.

items.title: Scientists discover a compound that could supercharge aging muscle repair
items.excerpt: A sulfur-based compound called LASSS appears to protect and supercharge a key protein involved in repairing damaged muscle. The discovery could eventually lead to new ways to slow muscle loss and preserve strength as people age.

items.title: A new antiviral could stop measles before an outbreak takes off
items.excerpt: An experimental pill prevented a measles-like virus from spreading through the air or through close contact in ferrets. It worked when given shortly before or after exposure and also shortened illness and the period when infected animals remained contagious. Researchers say the drug could one day complement vaccination and make outbreaks easier to contain.

items.title: The billion-dollar problem keeping Ozempic, Wegovy, and Zepbound from patients
items.excerpt: Ozempic, Wegovy, and similar drugs may deliver excellent health value, but covering them for millions of eligible patients could overwhelm insurance budgets. Researchers say better long-term support is needed and warn that low-cost compounded versions may carry serious safety risks.

items.title: APOE2 may protect the brain from Alzheimer’s and aging
items.excerpt: The longevity-linked APOE2 gene appears to protect brain cells by reducing DNA damage and helping neurons recover from stress. The finding could open a new path toward treatments that mimic APOE2’s defenses in people at higher genetic risk for Alzheimer’s.

items.title: Ozempic and Mounjaro linked to a surprising hair loss risk
items.excerpt: A large study suggests that GLP-1 drugs including Ozempic and Mounjaro may slightly increase the risk of hair loss in people with type 2 diabetes. Compared with two other common classes of diabetes medications, GLP-1 users had a significantly higher rate of alopecia, though the absolute risk was still low. The hair loss was mainly non-scarring, which means it may be reversible.

items.title: How Cinematographers Choose Their Grip Packages
items.excerpt: This video is sponsored by Squarespace: the all-in-one platform to build your website. From the largest swopping crane movements to the subtlest tracking shot, or even a shot mounted directly onto a vehicle, grips are responsible. Grip gear is the backbone of executing any safe and efficient camera motion or rigging a complex lighting setup. As a cinematographer who has worked on both small content shoots or indie productions, as well as industry sized feature film or series shoots, I’ll take you through a typical grips gear package often selected by DPs for both of these different scales of production. From head and legs, to dollies, gimbals and even grips gear for special occasions, let's get started. The one piece of gear that pretty much any production needs is a tripod. But not all tripods are the same. They come in different shapes and sizes and are mainly selected for the payload they can take. In other words, how big is the camera. For our indie shoot, which I’ve assumed will use something like a Sony FX6 or smaller, there are many different options but the tripod kit I’ve listed is the Cartoni Focus 12 with a payload of 12kg. This kit comes with a 100mm bowl tripod head which can be detached and legs that can adjust the tripod height or spread to get lower. To cover any low angles, where the camera needs to get very low to the ground, I’ve included a hi hat, a miniature tripod base which the 100mm tripod head can fit into. Because industry cinema cameras and lenses are larger and heavier, they need a tripod with a larger payload. The industry standard is the O’Connor 2575 fluid head designed for cameras up to 40.8kg - which supports basically any camera and heavy cinema zoom lens. This industry head is paired with heavy duty aluminium legs, which come in a short size, for a lower height, or tall legs for a higher angle shot. Unlike our indie tripod, which can be levelled by adjusting the round bowl, these heavy production tripods are bubbled by adjusting the individual height of each leg until the horizon is level. With a hit-hat, I’ve also included a steadybag, which is like a beanbag that gets the camera even lower by sitting it directly on the ground

items.title: Sweden’s hidden rare earth deposits could help break China’s grip
items.excerpt: Sweden could become an important source of rare earth elements needed for magnets and green technologies. Instead of extracting one target metal, researchers are cataloging everything contained in Swedish mineral deposits. They will then develop magnets using those naturally available combinations, potentially reducing pollution, waste, and reliance on China.

items.excerpt: China’s Tianwen-1 orbiter captured a faint but remarkable view of 3I/ATLAS as the interstellar comet raced past Mars. The images reveal its tail and glowing coma, offering scientists a glimpse of material born beyond our solar system.

items.excerpt: A newly identified fossil ape from northern Egypt is reshaping ideas about where the ancestors of modern apes first emerged. items.title: New giant salamander species discovered in 3.5-million-year-old fossils
items.excerpt: Fossils discovered in Japan more than two decades ago have finally been identified as a new genus and species of giant salamander. Limnospondylus ajimuensis lived around 3.5 million years ago in a warmer, wetter Kyushu and may have reached 1.1 meters in length. Its discovery hints that many more unknown giant salamanders could be hiding among fossils previously given the wrong classification.
